sections.text: Welcome to AP Biology, Module Sixteen — Exam Synthesis and Review. This is the capstone. There's no new biology today. Instead we take everything from all eight units and organize it the way the AP exam will actually test you. Two days before the big game, this is the film session.

Here's what almost everyone gets wrong about the AP Biology exam: they study like it's a vocabulary test. But look at the scale — half your score is free response, and that half is data reasoning. Reading an experiment, analyzing a graph, building an argument from evidence. You can memorize every term in the book and still leave half the points on the table. This module fixes that.

Three things today. One — how the exam is built: two sections, the one-to-five scale, and the six science practices hiding inside every question. Two — free-response technique: how to read an experiment, state a hypothesis, and write a claim that earns points. Three — a rapid recap of the cross-unit traps that cost the most points every year, and exactly how to spend your final thirty days.

The exam: two sections, three hours total, split exactly fifty-fifty. Section One — sixty multiple-choice questions in ninety minutes, half your score; many come in sets attached to data and graphs. Section Two — six free-response questions, also ninety minutes, the other half. Two are long, eight to ten points each, where you interpret a whole experiment. The other four are short, four points each — analyze data, use a model, or predict an outcome.

Your raw points convert to one number on a one-to-five scale. A three or higher is a qualifying score — it earns college credit at many institutions, and that's the bar most students aim for. Just know each college sets its own policy: some accept a three, some want a four or five, so check the schools on your list. The good news — you don't need a perfect paper to clear a three.

Behind every question are six science practices. One — explain a concept in your own words. Two — read a model or diagram and say what it shows. Three — design an experiment, naming the independent variable, dependent variable, and control. Four — turn data into a graph or table with labeled axes. Five — run a statistical test, like a chi-square or error bars, and interpret it. Six — argue from evidence. When a question feels strange, ask which practice it's testing.

The move on every free response. Step one — read the experiment: identify the independent variable, dependent variable, and control. Step two — state a testable hypothesis: if light increases, then photosynthesis rate increases, because more light drives the light reactions — directional, not a vague restatement. Step three — argue from evidence: make the claim, cite the actual data trend, and explain the biology, connecting molecular detail up to the ecological picture. That connection is where synthesis points live.

This is the trap that costs the most points on data questions. Correlation means two variables move together — towns that sell more ice cream report more drownings. But ice cream doesn't cause drowning; a lurking variable, summer heat, drives both. Causation needs a manipulated variable plus a control group. So on a free response, describe the relationship — X is positively associated with Y — and only claim causation when a variable was actually controlled and manipulated.

A classic Unit Eight trap. Exponential growth is the J-curve — d-N d-t equals r-N — assuming unlimited resources, so it just accelerates: bacteria in fresh medium, an invasive species with no predators. Logistic growth is the S-curve — d-N d-t equals r-N times K minus N over K — where growth slows near carrying capacity, K. Two cautions: K isn't a hard ceiling, so populations can overshoot and oscillate around it; and don't mix up the curves on a graph-ID question.

Your turn. A researcher surveys thirty lakes. Lakes with higher dissolved phosphorus also have higher algae density — a strong, positive relationship. Write the FRQ-safe conclusion. Can you say phosphorus caused the algae bloom? And if not, what would the experiment need to add before you could? Pause now. Write your sentence. Press play when you're ready.

Here's the FRQ-safe answer. Dissolved phosphorus is positively correlated with algae density across the thirty lakes. Notice the wording — we describe the relationship, we do not say it caused the bloom. Why not? This is observational: no variable manipulated, no control, and a lurking variable like agricultural runoff could drive both. To claim causation you'd need a controlled experiment — manipulate phosphorus across replicate mesocosms, hold everything else constant, include a no-added-phosphorus control, and look for a dose-response. That's claim, evidence, reasoning.

A rapid round of cross-unit traps. The ten-percent rule — about ten percent of energy moves up a trophic level, ninety percent lost as heat; energy flows one way, matter cycles. Hardy-Weinberg is a null model — p-squared plus two-p-q plus q-squared equals one predicts no evolution only if all five assumptions hold: no mutation, no gene flow, no drift, random mating, no selection. Evolution basics — fitness is reproductive success; selection acts on phenotypes; drift is random; homologous means common ancestor, analogous means convergent.

How to spend your last thirty days. Week one — diagnose: take one full timed practice exam, both sections, score it honestly, rank your three weakest of the eight units, and re-watch only those. Week two — drill: every other day do one or two released free-response questions under time, writing full claim-evidence-reasoning answers, plus multiple-choice data sets. Week three — simulate, then rest: one more full timed exam early, run the trap list once more, then light review and sleep.

Recap. Structure — Section One, sixty multiple choice, ninety minutes, fifty percent; Section Two, six free response, ninety minutes, fifty percent, two long worth eight to ten points and four short worth four. Scored one to five; a three-plus earns credit at many colleges. Six science practices behind every question. The free-response move — read the experiment, state a testable hypothesis, then claim, evidence, reasoning. Traps — correlation isn't causation, exponential versus logistic, the ten-percent rule, Hardy-Weinberg as a null model, fitness as reproduction.

And that's it — you've finished all sixteen modules of AP Biology. There is no next module. Here's your final step. Take a full timed practice exam under real conditions — both sections, three hours — using a recent released exam from The College Board or Khan Academy. Then book an advisor review session through the GIIS dashboard and bring your scored exam, so we can target your weakest units. You've done the work — now go earn the score. I'm proud of you.
